issues.description: The original code used `.unwrap_or(text)` on the quote-stripping chain, with an explicit comment: '// Strip outer quotes safely (error recovery can produce malformed nodes)'. The diff replaces this with `.unwrap()`, which panics whenever tree-sitter produces a string node missing its outer quotes — a documented and expected condition during error recovery. The existing test `unclosed_string_does_not_panic()` in `crates/reify-syntax/tests/edge_case_tests.rs` (~line 55) was written specifically to guard this path and will now fail (or the test infrastructure catches the panic and masks the regression).
issues.suggested_fix: Revert `.unwrap()` back to `.unwrap_or(text)` to preserve the documented error-recovery fallback. The comment must stay. If the intent is to tighten behavior, add an explicit test that proves no tree-sitter node ever reaches this function without proper delimiters — which requires evidence from the grammar, not an assumption.

issues.description: `lower_single_quoted_string` uses `.unwrap()` with no fallback, mirroring the same bug introduced in `lower_string_literal`. There are zero tests for this new function — not a single happy-path test, no empty-string test, no error-recovery test. Additionally, the tree-sitter grammar in `tree-sitter-reify/grammar.js` only defines double-quoted string literals; if no grammar rule ever produces a single-quoted node, this function is unreachable dead code, and the fact that it is untested means no one caught this.
issues.suggested_fix: Before merging: (1) Add at least a happy-path test and an unterminated/malformed-input test for `lower_single_quoted_string`. (2) Verify that the grammar actually emits a node type routed to this function; if it does not, remove the function. (3) Replace `.unwrap()` with `.unwrap_or(text)` (same recovery pattern as the original `lower_string_literal`) to be safe against error-recovery nodes.

issues.description: The test `unclosed_string_does_not_panic()` was written to verify the error-recovery path now removed by this diff. With `.unwrap()` in place the test either (a) panics and fails, exposing the bug, or (b) tree-sitter's own recovery prevents a bare-quote node from ever reaching `lower_string_literal`, silently making the test vacuous. Either outcome should be understood before merging — the test may need to be updated to reflect whatever the new contract actually is.
issues.suggested_fix: Run the test suite and confirm `unclosed_string_does_not_panic` passes. If it does pass, add a comment explaining why `.unwrap()` is safe (e.g., 'tree-sitter grammar guarantees well-formed nodes here'). If it panics, revert the `.unwrap()` change.

issues.description: Even if the grammar does route nodes here, no tests cover: empty single-quoted string `''`, a string containing a double quote `'say "hi"'`, embedded backslash escapes, or the error-recovery scenario of an unterminated single-quoted string. These are the same classes of cases already tested for double-quoted strings.
issues.suggested_fix: Add parallel tests in `edge_case_tests.rs` mirroring the existing `string_literal_normal`, `empty_string_literal`, and `unclosed_string_does_not_panic` tests but for single-quoted input.

test_analyst.summary: The diff makes two related changes: it replaces an intentional `.unwrap_or(text)` error-recovery fallback in `lower_string_literal` with a bare `.unwrap()`, and it introduces a new `lower_single_quoted_string` function that has the same `.unwrap()` pattern and zero test coverage. The original code carried an explicit comment explaining why the fallback was needed ("error recovery can produce malformed nodes"), and a dedicated test `unclosed_string_does_not_panic()` guards exactly this path. Replacing the fallback with `.unwrap()` turns documented defensive behavior into a panic on any tree-sitter error-recovery node that lacks proper quote delimiters. Both changes are blocking: the first regresses a tested safety property, the second ships completely untested code that panics on predictable bad input. Additionally, the grammar (`grammar.js`) only defines double-quoted string literals, raising the question of whether `lower_single_quoted_string` is ever reachable at all — a concern that only a test (or a grammar-routing audit) can answer.

issues.description: lower_single_quoted_string is an almost exact copy of lower_string_literal — both get node text, strip a matching open/close quote character, call .unwrap().to_string(), and return the same Some(Expr { kind: ExprKind::StringLiteral(s), span: self.span(node) }). The only difference is the delimiter char (" vs '). This violates DRY: if the error-recovery strategy changes (e.g. reverting .unwrap() back to .unwrap_or(text)), it must be updated in two places. The original lower_string_literal even had a comment explaining the safety rationale ('error recovery can produce malformed nodes') that is absent in the new clone.

reuse_auditor.summary: The diff introduces lower_single_quoted_string as a near-verbatim duplicate of lower_string_literal, differing only in the quote delimiter character. All logic — stripping prefix/suffix, .unwrap().to_string(), and constructing the same Expr variant — is copy-pasted. A single parameterised private helper would eliminate the duplication and ensure any future changes to quote-stripping or error-recovery strategy are made in one place. No other reuse issues were found in the diff.

issues.description: The original `.unwrap_or(text)` was explicitly annotated 'Strip outer quotes safely (error recovery can produce malformed nodes)'. Replacing it with `.unwrap()` will panic at runtime whenever tree-sitter's error-recovery produces a string node that lacks its surrounding quotes — a well-known and documented occurrence. The existing test `unclosed_string_does_not_panic` in edge_case_tests.rs is designed to catch exactly this scenario and will now fail. The function's `Option<Expr>` return type implies graceful fallibility, but `.unwrap()` makes it a hidden panic site instead.
issues.suggested_fix: Restore `.unwrap_or(text)` — the pre-existing pattern used in at least 4 other identical strip_prefix/strip_suffix chains in this file (lines 644-646, 1661-1664, etc.). If a malformed node must be signalled rather than silently recovered, push a diagnostic and return `None`, consistent with the check_and_lower! macro pattern used elsewhere.

issues.description: The new `lower_single_quoted_string` also uses `.unwrap()` on the strip_prefix/strip_suffix chain. Tree-sitter error-recovery nodes for single-quoted strings can equally lack their surrounding quotes. This function will panic on malformed input, violating the same invariant broken in `lower_string_literal` above.
issues.suggested_fix: Use `.unwrap_or(text)` (or push a diagnostic and return `None`) rather than `.unwrap()`, matching the established error-recovery convention in the file.

issues.description: `lower_single_quoted_string` is a near-exact clone of `lower_string_literal`, differing only in the quote character. This duplicates the body, the span construction, and the ExprKind wrapping. The codebase already factors similar parameterisations (e.g. `lower_quoted_literal` style helpers) as private helpers. Two identical implementations will diverge over time (e.g. one gets escape-sequence handling, the other does not).
issues.suggested_fix: Extract a private helper `fn lower_quoted_literal(&self, node: tree_sitter::Node, open: char, close: char) -> Option<Expr>` and have both `lower_string_literal` and `lower_single_quoted_string` delegate to it.

issues.description: The new `lower_single_quoted_string` function is not wired into `lower_expr`'s match dispatch in this diff. If the grammar node kind for single-quoted strings (e.g. `"single_quoted_string"`) has not been added to the match arms in `lower_expr`, this function will never be called and the compiler will emit a dead-code warning — or the feature will silently be absent at runtime.
issues.suggested_fix: Add the corresponding match arm in `lower_expr` (e.g. `"single_quoted_string" => self.lower_single_quoted_string(node)`) in the same diff, and add a test that exercises parsing a single-quoted string literal end-to-end.

architect_reviewer.summary: The diff has two blocking regressions and two architectural concerns. The core problem is replacing `.unwrap_or(text)` with `.unwrap()` in both `lower_string_literal` (an existing function whose comment explicitly documented the fallback as intentional error-recovery) and the newly added `lower_single_quoted_string`. Tree-sitter's error-recovery routinely produces string nodes that lack their surrounding quote characters; `.unwrap()` will panic on these inputs, directly breaking the no-panic contract that the existing `unclosed_string_does_not_panic` test enforces and that every other quote-stripping call site in the file upholds via `.unwrap_or(raw)`. Beyond the runtime safety issues, the new function is an almost verbatim duplicate of `lower_string_literal` (DRY violation) and appears not to be connected to `lower_expr`'s match dispatch in this diff, risking dead code or a silently missing feature. All four issues can be resolved by restoring the `.unwrap_or` fallback, extracting a shared `lower_quoted_literal` helper, and wiring the new function into the expression-lowering dispatch.

issues.description: The diff replaces `.unwrap_or(text)` with `.unwrap()` in `lower_string_literal`, eliminating the error-recovery path that the original comment explicitly documents: "error recovery can produce malformed nodes". Tree-sitter uses error-recovery to produce partial parse trees for syntactically invalid or incomplete input — exactly what happens on every keystroke in an LSP scenario. A string literal with a missing closing quote (e.g., `foo = "bar`) will cause `strip_suffix('"')` to return `None`, and `.unwrap()` will panic. This crashes the parser thread (or the entire language server process if not caught) instead of gracefully degrading. Since `lower_string_literal` is on the hot path — dispatched for every string literal at line 1697 — and LSP re-parses on each edit, this is a reliability-critical regression in a hot path.
issues.suggested_fix: Restore the error-recovery fallback: `.unwrap_or(text).to_string()`. If the intent is to distinguish malformed nodes, return `None` and push a parse error instead: `.ok_or(()).map_err(|_| self.push_error("malformed string literal".into(), self.span(node))).ok()?.to_string()`.

issues.description: The newly introduced `lower_single_quoted_string` has the same `.unwrap()` without error-recovery fallback. A single-quoted string with a missing closing quote — again, normal during incremental editing — will panic.
issues.suggested_fix: Use `.unwrap_or(text)` or `?`-operator with an explicit `None` return, consistent with how other `lower_*` functions handle missing/malformed children (they return `None`).

issues.description: `lower_single_quoted_string` is a verbatim copy of `lower_string_literal` with only the delimiter character changed. Each duplicate independently allocates a `String` via `.to_string()`. Maintaining two near-identical functions increases the risk of divergence (e.g., one getting a performance improvement like string interning while the other does not), and doubles the surface area for the hot-path allocation logic.
issues.suggested_fix: Extract a shared helper: `fn lower_quoted_string_inner(&self, node: tree_sitter::Node, quote: char) -> Option<Expr>` that strips the supplied delimiter and constructs the `Expr`. Both public methods become one-liner wrappers, and the allocation sits in a single place that is easy to optimize (e.g., string interning) later.

performance.summary: The diff introduces two `blocking` regressions in the parser hot path. The original `lower_string_literal` deliberately used `.unwrap_or(text)` as error recovery because tree-sitter produces malformed nodes for incomplete input (a documented invariant in the code comment). Replacing this with `.unwrap()` — and repeating the same mistake in the new `lower_single_quoted_string` — means any string literal missing its closing quote will panic the parser rather than gracefully recover. In an LSP scenario where the parser is invoked on every keystroke, this is a crash-on-edit regression. Additionally, the two functions are near-identical, creating duplicated allocation logic and a maintenance hazard for future hot-path optimizations (such as string interning).

issues.description: The diff replaces the intentionally defensive `.unwrap_or(text)` with `.unwrap()` in `lower_string_literal`. The removed comment explicitly stated 'error recovery can produce malformed nodes'. Because `lower_expr` dispatches to this function without pre-filtering ERROR/MISSING nodes (no `is_error()` / `has_error()` guard), a tree-sitter error-recovery node for an unterminated string literal (e.g. the user is mid-edit: `let x = "hello`) will produce node text without a closing quote. `strip_suffix('"')` returns `None`, and `.unwrap()` panics. Two other identical patterns in the same file (`lower_field_source` and `lower_meta_block`) both retain `.unwrap_or(raw)` for exactly this reason.
issues.suggested_fix: Restore the original fallback: `.unwrap_or(text).to_string()`. If a stricter error is desired, push a diagnostic and return `None` rather than panicking.

issues.description: The new `lower_single_quoted_string` function has the same problem: it uses `.unwrap()` on the result of `strip_prefix('\'').and_then(|s| s.strip_suffix('\''))`. An unterminated single-quoted string (`let x = 'hello`) will cause tree-sitter to produce a node without a closing quote during error recovery, `strip_suffix` returns `None`, and `.unwrap()` panics. The function is called from the same expression-lowering path that performs no upstream error-node filtering.
issues.suggested_fix: Use `.unwrap_or(text).to_string()` consistent with the project pattern, or push a diagnostic and return `None` on `None`.

robustness.summary: Both changes in this diff introduce unconditional `.unwrap()` calls on `Option` values that are `None` whenever a string literal node lacks its closing quote — a routine situation under tree-sitter's error recovery. The original `lower_string_literal` deliberately used `.unwrap_or(text)` and carried a comment calling out this exact hazard; the diff silently removes the comment and the guard together. The new `lower_single_quoted_string` repeats the same mistake. Because `lower_expr` dispatches to these functions without any `is_error()` / `has_error()` pre-filter, any incomplete string literal in user source (e.g. during live editing in the language server) will reach these `.unwrap()` calls and panic. Two analogous sites in the same file (`lower_field_source`, `lower_meta_block`) both use `.unwrap_or(raw)`, confirming the safe pattern is well-established. Both issues are blocking: they will cause process panics on valid, expected parser inputs.
